Tracking utilization trends over time and setting alarms at 70-80% of capacity thresholds enables proactive capacity planning—adding attachments or upgrading bandwidth before saturation causes performance issues.
Option1: Monitor Transit Gateway CloudWatch metrics with trend analysis and capacity threshold alarms for proactive planning
Option2: Check Transit Gateway utilization manually each month
Option3: Assume Transit Gateway has unlimited capacity
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network access logging for compliance showing all accepted and rejected traffic in their VPC. Which configuration provides this?
Explanation: VPC Flow Logs with the log type set to ALL capture both accepted and rejected traffic. The log records include source/destination IPs, ports, protocol, packets, bytes, and the action (ACCEPT/REJECT). Publishing to S3 provides cost-effective long-term storage. Publishing to CloudWatch Logs enables real-time analysis. Both accepted and rejected traffic are needed for complete compliance audit trails.
Option1: Configure VPC Flow Logs with ALL traffic type published to both S3 for archival and CloudWatch Logs for analysis
Option2: Log only accepted traffic
Option3: Log only rejected traffic
Option4: Use CloudTrail for network traffic logging

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated incident response when Network Firewall detects an intrusion attempt. Which architecture provides this?
Explanation: Network Firewall publishes alert logs to CloudWatch Logs. A CloudWatch Logs subscription filter matches IPS alert patterns and triggers a Lambda function. The Lambda function creates a Security Hub finding, sends an SNS alert, and can automatically update Network Firewall rules to block the attacking IP. This provides automated detection, alerting, and response for network intrusion attempts.
Option1: Network Firewall alerts to CloudWatch Logs triggering Lambda for automated blocking alerting and Security Hub finding creation
Option2: Review Network Firewall logs manually
Option3: Rely on Network Firewall blocking without alerting
Option4: Use security groups for intrusion response

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for a multi-Region active-active application with automatic traffic routing to the nearest healthy Region. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: Global Accelerator with endpoints in multiple Regions provides automatic routing to the nearest healthy endpoint. Health checks detect Regional failures and route traffic to healthy Regions. Unlike Route 53 (which depends on DNS TTL for failover), Global Accelerator routes at the network layer with near-instant failover. Two static anycast IPs simplify client configuration.
Option1: Use AWS Global Accelerator with multi-Region endpoints and health checks for automatic nearest-Region routing
Option2: Use Route 53 simple routing to a single Region
Option3: Use CloudFront for active-active routing
Option4: Deploy in a single Region only

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for workloads that require dedicated single-tenant hardware for compliance. Which EC2 feature provides this?
Explanation: EC2 Dedicated Hosts provide physical servers dedicated to a single customer. The customer has visibility into the physical host (sockets, cores) and can control instance placement. This meets compliance requirements that mandate single-tenant hardware (like some financial and government regulations). Dedicated Instances provide single-tenant hardware without host-level visibility or control.
Option1: Use EC2 Dedicated Hosts for single-tenant hardware with full host visibility and placement control
Option2: Use standard shared-tenancy instances
Option3: Use Spot Instances for dedicated hardware
Option4: Use Lambda for dedicated compute

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides the lowest cost for transferring large amounts of data between AWS Regions. Which approach minimizes cross-Region data transfer costs?
Explanation: S3 Transfer costs between Regions are charged at standard cross-Region data transfer rates. For large datasets, compressing data before transfer reduces the volume (and cost). Using S3 Replication with S3 Replication Time Control ensures timely delivery. For very large one-time transfers, Snowball Edge can be used. For ongoing transfers, Direct Connect with inter-Region peering provides consistent pricing.
Option1: Compress data before cross-Region transfer and use S3 Replication for ongoing transfers to minimize data volume and cost
Option2: Transfer uncompressed data over the internet
Option3: Use individual S3 GET/PUT operations for each object
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for an application that requires both public and private subnets with controlled internet access. Which is the standard three-tier VPC architecture?
Explanation: The standard three-tier VPC has: public subnets (with Internet Gateway route) for ALBs and bastion hosts; private subnets (with NAT Gateway route) for application servers that need outbound internet; isolated subnets (no internet route) for databases. Security groups and NACLs control traffic between tiers. This provides defense in depth with appropriate internet exposure per tier.
Option1: Public subnets for load balancers private subnets with NAT for app servers and isolated subnets for databases
Option2: All resources in public subnets
Option3: All resources in a single subnet
Option4: No subnets with direct internet access

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network address preservation when migrating on-premises workloads to AWS. Applications use hardcoded IP addresses that cannot be changed. Which approach preserves IP addresses?
Explanation: AWS allows bringing your own IP addresses (BYOIP) to use in AWS. The engineer provisions their on-premises IP range in AWS and assigns these IPs to EC2 instances. Applications continue using the same IP addresses after migration. Alternatively, for private IPs, the VPC CIDR can match the on-premises subnet, and instances can be assigned the same private IPs.
Option1: Use BYOIP to bring on-premises public IP ranges to AWS or match VPC CIDR to on-premises subnets for private IP preservation
Option2: Assign new IP addresses and update all applications
Option3: Use NAT to translate between old and new IPs
Option4: IP addresses cannot be preserved during migration

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for a containerized application on EKS where each pod needs its own IP address from the VPC CIDR. Which CNI plugin provides this?
Explanation: The Amazon VPC CNI plugin assigns each pod a real VPC IP address from the subnet CIDR. This enables pods to communicate with other VPC resources (RDS, ElastiCache) using native VPC networking without NAT or overlay networks. Pods appear as regular VPC endpoints with security group support. Prefix delegation mode increases the number of available pod IPs per node.
Option1: Use the Amazon VPC CNI plugin which assigns real VPC IP addresses to each pod
Option2: Use a custom overlay network CNI
Option3: Share the node's IP address among all pods
Option4: Use host networking for all pods

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their ALB supports gRPC traffic for their microservices application. Which ALB configuration enables this?
Explanation: ALB supports gRPC natively through HTTP/2. gRPC uses HTTP/2 as its transport protocol. The ALB listener is configured with an HTTPS listener (gRPC requires TLS). The target group protocol version is set to gRPC. ALB performs health checks using gRPC health checking protocol. This enables gRPC load balancing with ALB's full feature set (path-based routing, health checks, access logs).
Option1: Configure ALB with HTTPS listener and gRPC protocol version on the target group
Option2: Use NLB for gRPC traffic
Option3: gRPC is not supported on AWS load balancers
Option4: Use a custom proxy for gRPC

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their VPC supports DNS resolution for a private hosted zone that is shared across multiple VPCs in different accounts. Which configuration enables this?
Explanation: Route 53 private hosted zones can be associated with VPCs in different accounts using the CreateVPCAssociationAuthorization API (in the hosted zone's account) and AssociateVPCWithHostedZone API (in the VPC's account). Once associated, instances in the VPC can resolve DNS records in the private hosted zone. This enables centralized DNS management with cross-account resolution.
Option1: Associate the private hosted zone with VPCs in other accounts using cross-account VPC association authorization
Option2: Create duplicate hosted zones in each account
Option3: Use public hosted zones for cross-account DNS
Option4: Configure custom DNS servers in each VPC

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their Transit Gateway attachment uses a specific AZ for traffic routing to optimize costs. Which Transit Gateway feature controls this?
Explanation: Transit Gateway appliance mode routes all traffic for a connection through a single AZ (the AZ of the appliance). Without appliance mode, Transit Gateway uses ECMP across AZs, which can cause asymmetric routing for stateful appliances. Appliance mode ensures symmetric routing through the same AZ, which is required for stateful firewalls and other inline security appliances.
Option1: Enable Transit Gateway appliance mode on the inspection VPC attachment for symmetric routing through stateful appliances
Option2: Use default Transit Gateway routing without appliance mode
Option3: Deploy appliances in all AZs without appliance mode
Option4: Use VPC peering instead of Transit Gateway

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their CloudFront distribution uses Lambda@Edge to modify request headers before forwarding to the origin. Which CloudFront event should they use?
Explanation: Lambda@Edge supports four event types: viewer-request (before cache check), origin-request (after cache miss, before origin), origin-response (after origin response, before caching), and viewer-response (before returning to viewer). For modifying headers before the origin receives the request, the origin-request event is appropriate—it runs only on cache misses, reducing Lambda invocations and cost.
Option1: Use Lambda@Edge on the origin-request event to modify headers before forwarding to the origin
Option2: Use the viewer-request event for origin header modification
Option3: Use the viewer-response event
Option4: Use the origin-response event

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their Direct Connect connection supports BGP communities for traffic engineering. Which BGP community values does AWS support?
Explanation: AWS Direct Connect supports BGP communities for route preference. The engineer can tag routes with community values to influence routing: 7224:9100 (local Region preference), 7224:9200 (continent preference), 7224:9300 (global preference). AWS also supports NO_EXPORT community to prevent route propagation. These communities enable fine-grained traffic engineering over Direct Connect.
Option1: Use AWS-supported BGP community values (7224:9100 9200 9300) for Direct Connect traffic engineering
Option2: Use arbitrary community values
Option3: BGP communities are not supported on Direct Connect
Option4: Use static routing instead of BGP

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their VPC supports multiple network interfaces on a single EC2 instance for network segmentation. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: EC2 instances support multiple ENIs (Elastic Network Interfaces), each in a different subnet with its own security group. This enables network segmentation—one ENI for management traffic (in a management subnet) and another for application traffic (in an application subnet). Each ENI has its own private IP and can have different security group rules. The number of ENIs depends on the instance type.
Option1: Attach multiple ENIs to the instance each in a different subnet with separate security groups for network segmentation
Option2: Use a single ENI with multiple IP addresses
Option3: Use VLANs within the VPC
Option4: Multiple ENIs are not supported on EC2

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated testing of their network security controls to verify that security groups and NACLs are correctly configured. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: VPC Reachability Analyzer can be used programmatically (via API) to test connectivity between specific endpoints. By creating a test suite that verifies expected connectivity (app tier can reach database) and expected blocking (internet cannot reach database), the engineer validates security controls after every change. This can be integrated into CI/CD pipelines for infrastructure changes.
Option1: Use VPC Reachability Analyzer programmatically in CI/CD pipelines to validate security controls after infrastructure changes
Option2: Test security controls manually after each change
Option3: Assume security controls are correct without testing
Option4: Use ping tests only

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides real-time notification when a VPC route table is modified. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: EventBridge captures CloudTrail API calls for route table modifications (CreateRoute, DeleteRoute, ReplaceRoute) in near real-time. An EventBridge rule matching these API calls triggers SNS notification and optionally Lambda for automated validation or remediation. This provides immediate awareness of route table changes that could affect network connectivity or security.
Option1: EventBridge rules on CloudTrail route table modification API calls for real-time notification
Option2: Check route tables manually each day
Option3: Use VPC Flow Logs for route table monitoring
Option4: Use AWS Config only for route table changes

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network performance benchmarking between their VPC and on-premises network. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Network performance testing tools (like iperf3) running on EC2 instances and on-premises servers measure actual throughput, latency, and packet loss across the network path. CloudWatch metrics for Direct Connect and VPN provide connection-level performance data. Regular benchmarking establishes baselines and detects performance degradation. AWS Network Manager provides ongoing network health monitoring.
Option1: Run network performance tests (iperf3) between EC2 and on-premises with CloudWatch metrics for ongoing monitoring
Option2: Assume network performance is constant
Option3: Use only ping for performance testing
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated documentation of their security group rules across all VPCs for audit purposes. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: AWS Config records all security group configurations and changes over time. The Config API (or Athena queries on Config snapshots in S3) can generate a complete inventory of all security group rules across all VPCs and accounts. This provides always-current documentation that auditors can review. Config's timeline feature shows the history of each security group's rule changes.
Option1: Use AWS Config to maintain automated always-current documentation of all security group rules across VPCs
Option2: Document security group rules manually in a spreadsheet
Option3: Use VPC Flow Logs for security group documentation
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network-level access control for their Lambda functions running in a VPC. Which TWO controls should they configure? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Security groups attached to the Lambda function's ENI control outbound traffic (Lambda in VPC only has outbound rules since it doesn't accept inbound connections). NACLs on the Lambda function's subnet provide an additional stateless filtering layer. Together they control which resources the Lambda function can communicate with at the network level, implementing defense in depth.
Option1: Configure security groups on the Lambda function's ENI for outbound traffic control
Option2: Configure NACLs on the Lambda function's subnet for additional filtering
Option3: Use IAM policies for network-level access control
Option4: Lambda functions cannot have network controls

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides protection against IP spoofing within their VPC. Which AWS feature prevents this?
Explanation: AWS VPC automatically prevents IP spoofing—instances can only send traffic with their assigned IP addresses as the source. The hypervisor drops any packet with a source IP that doesn't match the instance's ENI. This is enforced at the infrastructure level and cannot be disabled (except by disabling source/destination check for NAT instances and firewalls). No configuration is needed.
Option1: AWS VPC automatically prevents IP spoofing at the hypervisor level—no configuration needed
Option2: Configure anti-spoofing rules in security groups
Option3: Use NACLs to prevent IP spoofing
Option4: IP spoofing prevention requires third-party tools

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides secure cross-account network connectivity without sharing VPC CIDRs or creating peering connections. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: AWS PrivateLink provides private connectivity between VPCs without VPC peering, Transit Gateway, or CIDR sharing. The provider creates an Endpoint Service (backed by NLB). The consumer creates an Interface Endpoint. Traffic flows through the AWS network using private IPs from the consumer's VPC. No CIDR overlap issues, no route table changes, and no transitive routing concerns.
Option1: Use AWS PrivateLink for secure cross-account connectivity without CIDR sharing or peering
Option2: Use VPC peering with CIDR sharing
Option3: Use Transit Gateway for all cross-account connectivity
Option4: Route traffic over the internet

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network forensics capability by capturing full packet data for security investigation. Which VPC feature provides this?
Explanation: VPC Traffic Mirroring captures full packet data (not just metadata like Flow Logs) from ENIs and sends copies to a target for analysis. Mirror filters select which traffic to capture (by protocol, port, direction). The mirrored traffic is sent to security appliances (IDS, packet analyzers) via an NLB or directly to an ENI. This enables deep packet inspection and forensic analysis of network traffic.
Option1: Use VPC Traffic Mirroring to capture full packet data for security forensics and deep packet inspection
Option2: Use VPC Flow Logs for packet capture
Option3: Use CloudTrail for network forensics
Option4: Full packet capture is not possible in AWS

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated compliance checking for their network architecture against AWS Well-Architected Framework security pillar recommendations. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: AWS Well-Architected Tool provides automated checks against the Well-Architected Framework pillars including security. For network-specific compliance, AWS Config rules check VPC configurations (Flow Logs enabled, default security group restricted, VPC endpoints configured). Security Hub's AWS Foundational Security Best Practices standard includes network security checks. Together they provide comprehensive network compliance assessment.
Option1: Use AWS Config rules and Security Hub for automated network security compliance checking against best practices
Option2: Manually review the architecture against the framework
Option3: Use CloudTrail for compliance checking
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for a disaster recovery site in a secondary Region with minimal ongoing cost. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: A pilot light DR architecture maintains minimal infrastructure in the DR Region—Transit Gateway, VPN/Direct Connect connectivity, and Route 53 health checks. VPC and subnet configurations are pre-deployed via CloudFormation. During a disaster, compute resources are launched from pre-configured launch templates. This minimizes ongoing costs (only networking infrastructure runs) while enabling rapid recovery.
Option1: Maintain pilot light networking infrastructure in the DR Region with pre-configured templates for rapid compute deployment
Option2: Run full production infrastructure in both Regions
Option3: Have no DR infrastructure and build from scratch during a disaster
Option4: Use only S3 replication for DR

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for a microservices application where services need to discover each other dynamically. Which AWS service provides service discovery?
Explanation: AWS Cloud Map provides service discovery for cloud resources. Services register with Cloud Map and other services query it to discover endpoints. Cloud Map supports DNS-based discovery (Route 53 private hosted zones) and API-based discovery. It integrates with ECS (automatic registration/deregistration) and EKS. This enables dynamic service discovery without hardcoded endpoints.
Option1: Use AWS Cloud Map for dynamic service discovery with DNS and API-based resolution
Option2: Hardcode service endpoints in configuration files
Option3: Use a load balancer for all service-to-service communication
Option4: Use Route 53 public hosted zones for service discovery

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their CloudFront distribution uses Origin Shield to reduce the load on their origin server. Which configuration enables this?
Explanation: CloudFront Origin Shield adds a centralized caching layer between Edge Locations and the origin. Without Origin Shield, each Edge Location independently requests content from the origin on cache misses. With Origin Shield, all Edge Locations request from Origin Shield first, which caches content and makes a single request to the origin. This dramatically reduces origin load and improves cache hit ratios.
Option1: Enable CloudFront Origin Shield in the Region closest to the origin for centralized caching
Option2: Increase the number of Edge Locations
Option3: Reduce the cache TTL
Option4: Disable caching entirely

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their NLB supports TLS termination with a certificate from ACM. Which configuration enables this?
Explanation: NLB supports TLS listeners that terminate TLS connections using ACM certificates. The TLS listener decrypts incoming traffic and forwards it to targets as TCP. This offloads TLS processing from backend instances. NLB TLS listeners support TLS 1.2 and 1.3 with configurable security policies. For end-to-end encryption, targets can also run TLS (NLB re-encrypts to the target).
Option1: Configure an NLB TLS listener with an ACM certificate for TLS termination
Option2: NLB does not support TLS termination
Option3: Use ALB for all TLS termination
Option4: Install certificates on each backend instance only

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated rollback of network configuration changes if they cause connectivity issues. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: CloudFormation with rollback triggers monitors CloudWatch Alarms during stack updates. If a network configuration change (route table, security group, NACL) causes a connectivity alarm to trigger, CloudFormation automatically rolls back the change. VPC Reachability Analyzer can be run as a post-change validation step, with failures triggering rollback. This provides automated safety nets for network changes.
Option1: Use CloudFormation rollback triggers with connectivity alarms and Reachability Analyzer validation for automated rollback
Option2: Apply network changes without any rollback capability
Option3: Manually revert changes if issues are detected
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network isolation for their RDS database ensuring it is only accessible from specific application subnets. Which TWO configurations enforce this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Placing RDS in a private subnet (no Internet Gateway route) prevents internet access. The RDS security group allowing inbound traffic only from the application subnet's security group (on the database port) restricts access to authorized application instances. Together they provide both network-level isolation (private subnet) and instance-level access control (security group) for the database.
Option1: Deploy RDS in a private subnet with no internet route
Option2: Configure the RDS security group to allow inbound only from the application security group
Option3: Place RDS in a public subnet with a restrictive security group
Option4: Use NACLs as the only access control

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated response when AWS Shield Advanced detects a DDoS attack. Which TWO automated responses should they configure? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Shield Advanced integrates with WAF for automatic rate-based rule activation during attacks—blocking attacking IPs automatically. Shield Advanced also integrates with CloudWatch for automated scaling—Auto Scaling policies triggered by Shield metrics add capacity to absorb the attack. Together they provide both blocking (WAF) and absorption (scaling) responses to DDoS attacks.
Option1: AWS WAF rate-based rules that automatically block attacking IPs during Shield-detected attacks
Option2: Auto Scaling policies triggered by Shield Advanced metrics to absorb attack traffic
Option3: Manually block IPs during attacks
Option4: Shut down the application during attacks

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for a multi-account environment where each business unit needs isolated network segments that can selectively communicate with shared services. Which Transit Gateway configuration provides this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Separate Transit Gateway route tables per business unit provide network isolation—each BU's VPCs can only see routes in their own route table. Route table associations and propagations for the shared services VPC are added to all BU route tables, enabling selective communication with shared services while maintaining BU isolation. Together these provide segmented connectivity with shared service access.
Option1: Separate Transit Gateway route tables per business unit for network isolation
Option2: Shared services VPC routes propagated to all business unit route tables for selective access
Option3: A single route table for all VPCs
Option4: VPC peering between all business units

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides the most cost-effective architecture for a development environment that needs VPC connectivity to shared services but minimal internet access. Which approach minimizes costs? (Select TWO)
Explanation: VPC peering (free for same-AZ traffic) between the dev VPC and shared services VPC avoids Transit Gateway costs ($0.05/GB + hourly attachment fees). A single NAT Gateway (instead of one per AZ) reduces NAT costs for development where high availability is less critical. Both optimizations reduce networking costs for non-production environments without impacting functionality.
Option1: Use VPC peering instead of Transit Gateway for dev-to-shared-services connectivity
Option2: Use a single NAT Gateway instead of one per AZ for development environments
Option3: Use Transit Gateway for all environments
Option4: Deploy NAT Gateways in every AZ for development

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for an application that requires both Layer 4 and Layer 7 load balancing. Which architecture should they use?
Explanation: An NLB in front of an ALB provides both Layer 4 (static IPs, TCP/UDP, ultra-low latency from NLB) and Layer 7 (path-based routing, host-based routing, WAF integration from ALB) capabilities. The NLB provides static IPs for whitelisting while the ALB provides advanced HTTP routing. AWS also supports this pattern natively with NLB targeting ALB IP addresses.
Option1: Use NLB in front of ALB to combine Layer 4 static IPs with Layer 7 HTTP routing capabilities
Option2: Use only ALB for all traffic
Option3: Use only NLB for all traffic
Option4: Use Classic Load Balancer for both layers

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for workloads that require access to AWS GovCloud from a commercial AWS account. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: AWS GovCloud is a separate partition from commercial AWS. Connectivity between commercial and GovCloud requires either VPN connections between VPCs in each partition or Direct Connect with separate VIFs for each partition. Transit Gateway peering does not work across partitions. The engineer must maintain separate networking infrastructure in each partition with explicit cross-partition connectivity.
Option1: Use VPN connections or Direct Connect with separate VIFs for cross-partition connectivity between commercial and GovCloud
Option2: Use Transit Gateway peering between commercial and GovCloud
Option3: Use VPC peering between partitions
Option4: GovCloud is accessible from commercial accounts by default

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their ALB supports weighted target groups for canary deployments. Which configuration enables gradual traffic shifting? (Select TWO)
Explanation: ALB forward actions support multiple target groups with configurable weights. A listener rule with a forward action specifying two target groups (current version weight 90, new version weight 10) distributes traffic proportionally. Adjusting weights gradually (90/10 → 70/30 → 50/50 → 0/100) implements a canary deployment. Health checks on both target groups ensure traffic only goes to healthy targets.
Option1: Configure ALB listener rules with weighted forward actions to multiple target groups
Option2: Use health checks on both target groups to ensure traffic goes only to healthy targets
Option3: Use Route 53 weighted routing instead of ALB
Option4: Deploy separate ALBs for each version

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their VPC supports AWS PrivateLink to expose an internal service to other VPCs and accounts. Which THREE components must they configure? (Select THREE)
Explanation: An NLB fronts the internal service (PrivateLink requires NLB as the service endpoint). A VPC Endpoint Service is created referencing the NLB, defining the service that consumers can connect to. Endpoint service permissions control which AWS accounts or principals can create Interface Endpoints to the service. All three are required to expose a service via PrivateLink.
Option1: Create an NLB fronting the internal service
Option2: Create a VPC Endpoint Service referencing the NLB
Option3: Configure endpoint service permissions to control which accounts can connect
Option4: Create an Internet Gateway for the service
Option5: Use ALB instead of NLB for PrivateLink

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their Direct Connect connection uses BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) for fast failure detection. Which configuration enables this?
Explanation: BFD on Direct Connect provides sub-second failure detection (compared to BGP's default 90-second hold timer). BFD is enabled on the customer's router for the BGP session over the Direct Connect VIF. AWS supports BFD on all VIF types. When BFD detects a link failure, it immediately notifies BGP, which withdraws routes and triggers failover to the backup path within seconds.
Option1: Enable BFD on the customer router for the Direct Connect BGP session for sub-second failure detection
Option2: Rely on BGP hold timer for failure detection
Option3: Use ICMP ping for failure detection
Option4: BFD is not supported on Direct Connect

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their Route 53 supports DNSSEC signing for their public hosted zone. Which configuration enables this?
Explanation: Route 53 supports DNSSEC signing for public hosted zones. The engineer enables DNSSEC signing on the hosted zone, which creates a KSK (Key Signing Key) in KMS. Route 53 generates the ZSK (Zone Signing Key) and signs all records. The DS (Delegation Signer) record must be added to the parent zone (domain registrar) to complete the chain of trust. This prevents DNS spoofing for the domain.
Option1: Enable DNSSEC signing on the Route 53 hosted zone and add the DS record to the parent zone
Option2: DNSSEC is not supported on Route 53
Option3: Use a third-party DNS for DNSSEC
Option4: DNSSEC is enabled by default

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their VPC supports prefix lists for simplified security group and route table management. Which feature provides this?
Explanation: Managed prefix lists are sets of CIDR blocks that can be referenced in security group rules and route table entries. Instead of adding individual CIDR rules, the engineer references the prefix list. When the prefix list is updated, all referencing security groups and route tables are automatically updated. AWS-managed prefix lists exist for services like S3 and DynamoDB. Custom prefix lists can be created and shared via RAM.
Option1: Use managed prefix lists referenced in security groups and route tables for simplified CIDR management
Option2: Add individual CIDR rules to each security group
Option3: Use NACLs instead of prefix lists
Option4: Prefix lists are not supported in AWS

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network change management with approval workflows before changes are applied to production. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: Infrastructure as code (CloudFormation/CDK) for all network changes stored in Git provides version control and code review. CloudFormation Change Sets preview changes before execution. A CI/CD pipeline with a manual approval step before production deployment ensures human review. This provides a complete change management workflow: propose → review → approve → apply → validate.
Option1: Use IaC in Git with CloudFormation Change Sets and CI/CD pipeline approval gates for network change management
Option2: Make network changes directly in the console
Option3: Apply changes without review or approval
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network inventory tracking across all VPCs accounts and Regions. Which TWO services provide this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Config records all VPC, subnet, security group, route table, and other network resource configurations across all accounts. AWS Network Manager discovers and visualizes Transit Gateways, Direct Connect, VPN, and VPC resources in a global topology map. Together they provide both detailed configuration inventory (Config) and visual topology mapping (Network Manager) for the entire network.
Option1: AWS Config for detailed network resource configuration inventory
Option2: AWS Network Manager for global network topology visualization
Option3: CloudTrail for network inventory
Option4: VPC Flow Logs for resource tracking

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides proactive monitoring of their VPC subnet IP address utilization to prevent IP exhaustion. Which approach should they use?
Explanation: CloudWatch metrics for VPC subnets (AvailableIPAddressCount) track the number of available IPs in each subnet. A CloudWatch Alarm triggers when available IPs drop below a threshold (e.g., 20% remaining), alerting the engineer to expand the subnet or add secondary CIDRs before IP exhaustion causes instance launch failures. This proactive monitoring prevents IP-related outages.
Option1: CloudWatch Alarms on subnet AvailableIPAddressCount metric for proactive IP exhaustion prevention
Option2: Check subnet IP availability manually
Option3: Assume subnets never run out of IPs
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network-level compliance with PCI DSS requirements for their payment processing application. Which THREE network controls are required? (Select THREE)
Explanation: Network segmentation isolates the cardholder data environment (CDE) from other systems using dedicated VPCs and Transit Gateway route tables. Encryption of cardholder data in transit (TLS) protects against eavesdropping. Network monitoring with VPC Flow Logs and Network Firewall IPS provides the intrusion detection and audit trail required by PCI DSS. All three are mandatory PCI DSS network requirements.
Option1: Network segmentation isolating the CDE with dedicated VPCs and Transit Gateway
Option2: Encryption of all cardholder data in transit with TLS
Option3: Network monitoring with VPC Flow Logs and Network Firewall IPS
Option4: Public internet access for CDE resources

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated security group rule analysis to identify overly permissive rules across all VPCs. Which TWO services provide this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: AWS Config with the restricted-common-ports rule identifies security groups allowing unrestricted access on sensitive ports. IAM Access Analyzer for security groups (via VPC Network Access Analyzer) identifies network paths that allow unintended access. Together they provide both rule-level analysis (Config) and path-level analysis (Network Access Analyzer) for comprehensive security group assessment.
Option1: AWS Config rules for identifying overly permissive security group rules
Option2: VPC Network Access Analyzer for identifying unintended network access paths
Option3: CloudTrail for security group analysis
Option4: VPC Flow Logs for rule analysis

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network access control for their S3 buckets ensuring they can only be accessed from specific VPCs. Which TWO configurations enforce this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: S3 bucket policies with the aws:sourceVpce condition restrict access to requests coming through specific VPC endpoints. VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 route traffic through the AWS private network. Together they ensure S3 is only accessible from authorized VPCs through their endpoints—not from the internet or other VPCs without endpoints. This provides network-level S3 access control.
Option1: S3 bucket policies with aws:sourceVpce condition restricting access to specific VPC endpoints
Option2: VPC Gateway Endpoints for S3 routing traffic through the private network
Option3: S3 Block Public Access only
Option4: Security groups on S3 buckets

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network-level protection against man-in-the-middle attacks for their Direct Connect connection. Which encryption option provides this?
Explanation: MACsec (IEEE 802.1AE) on Direct Connect provides Layer 2 point-to-point encryption between the customer router and the AWS Direct Connect endpoint. MACsec encrypts all traffic at the link layer, preventing eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks on the physical connection. MACsec is available on 10 Gbps and 100 Gbps dedicated connections. For additional protection, IPsec VPN over Direct Connect adds Layer 3 encryption.
Option1: Enable MACsec encryption on the Direct Connect connection for Layer 2 protection against MITM attacks
Option2: Rely on the physical security of the fiber only
Option3: Use security groups for MITM protection
Option4: Direct Connect traffic cannot be encrypted

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for a serverless application using API Gateway Lambda and DynamoDB with no internet exposure. Which architecture provides this?
Explanation: A private API Gateway endpoint (accessible only through VPC Interface Endpoints) eliminates internet exposure. Lambda functions in VPC private subnets access DynamoDB through a VPC Gateway Endpoint. No Internet Gateway, NAT Gateway, or public IPs are needed. All traffic stays within the AWS private network. This provides a fully private serverless architecture with zero internet exposure.
Option1: Private API Gateway with VPC endpoints plus Lambda in private subnets with DynamoDB Gateway Endpoint for zero internet exposure
Option2: Use a public API Gateway endpoint
Option3: Place Lambda in public subnets
Option4: Access DynamoDB over the internet

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for a multi-cloud architecture connecting AWS Azure and GCP through a central hub. Which approach provides this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Transit Gateway serves as the AWS hub connecting all AWS VPCs. Site-to-Site VPN connections from Transit Gateway to Azure VPN Gateway and GCP Cloud VPN provide encrypted cross-cloud connectivity. For higher bandwidth, Megaport or Equinix Cloud Exchange provide dedicated cross-cloud connectivity at colocation facilities where all three providers have presence. Both approaches enable multi-cloud networking through a central AWS hub.
Option1: Transit Gateway as the central hub with VPN connections to Azure and GCP
Option2: Cross-cloud connectivity services like Megaport at colocation facilities for higher bandwidth
Option3: VPC peering between AWS and other clouds
Option4: Direct Connect to Azure and GCP

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides the lowest possible network latency for a real-time gaming application. Which TWO EC2 networking features should they use? (Select TWO)
Explanation: Placement Groups (Cluster) place instances on the same rack for minimal network hops and lowest latency. Enhanced Networking with ENA (Elastic Network Adapter) provides up to 100 Gbps bandwidth with lower latency and jitter compared to standard networking. Together they provide the absolute lowest network latency available on EC2—essential for real-time gaming where microseconds matter.
Option1: Cluster Placement Group for minimal network hops between instances
Option2: Enhanced Networking with ENA for high bandwidth and low latency
Option3: Spread Placement Group for redundancy
Option4: Standard networking without ENA

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their Transit Gateway supports equal-cost multi-path routing across multiple Direct Connect connections for increased bandwidth. Which configuration enables this?
Explanation: Transit Gateway supports ECMP across multiple Direct Connect transit VIFs. By creating multiple Direct Connect connections (or using a LAG) with transit VIFs to the same Transit Gateway, traffic is distributed across all connections. BGP advertises the same prefixes across all connections with equal metrics. This provides aggregate bandwidth across multiple Direct Connect connections.
Option1: Configure multiple Direct Connect transit VIFs to the same Transit Gateway with BGP ECMP for aggregate bandwidth
Option2: Use a single Direct Connect connection only
Option3: Use VPN for bandwidth aggregation
Option4: ECMP is not supported with Direct Connect

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution where their VPC supports AWS Client VPN for remote workers to access VPC resources securely. Which TWO components must they configure? (Select TWO)
Explanation: A Client VPN endpoint is created with a server certificate (from ACM) and authentication configuration (AD, SAML, or mutual TLS). Authorization rules define which network CIDRs the VPN clients can access. The endpoint is associated with VPC subnets, creating ENIs that provide network connectivity. Together these enable secure remote access to VPC resources for remote workers.
Option1: Create a Client VPN endpoint with server certificate and authentication configuration
Option2: Configure authorization rules defining which networks VPN clients can access
Option3: Open port 22 in all security groups
Option4: Use an Internet Gateway for VPN access

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated compliance reporting for their network architecture showing all security groups NACLs and route tables across the organization. Which approach provides this?
Explanation: AWS Config with an aggregator collects resource configurations from all accounts. Config's advanced query feature enables SQL-like queries across all network resources (SELECT * WHERE resourceType = 'AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup'). Reports can be generated showing all security groups, NACLs, and route tables with their configurations. This provides automated, always-current compliance documentation.
Option1: Use AWS Config aggregator with advanced queries for automated cross-account network compliance reporting
Option2: Document network configurations manually
Option3: Use CloudTrail for configuration reporting
Option4: None of the above

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides automated DNS failover when a primary endpoint becomes unhealthy. The failover should occur within 30 seconds. Which Route 53 configuration provides the fastest failover?
Explanation: Route 53 health checks with a 10-second interval and a failure threshold of 1 (fail after 1 missed check) detect failures within 10-20 seconds. Fast health check intervals (10 seconds vs default 30) and low failure thresholds minimize detection time. Combined with a low TTL (60 seconds) on the DNS record, total failover time is approximately 30-90 seconds depending on client DNS caching.
Option1: Configure Route 53 health checks with 10-second intervals and failure threshold of 1 with low DNS TTL for fastest failover
Option2: Use default 30-second health check intervals
Option3: Set failure threshold to 10 for reliability
Option4: Use a 24-hour DNS TTL

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network-level access control for their ECS tasks running on Fargate. Which feature provides task-level network isolation?
Explanation: ECS Fargate tasks use the awsvpc network mode, giving each task its own ENI with a security group. The security group controls inbound and outbound traffic at the task level—different tasks can have different security groups with different rules. This provides network isolation between tasks even when they run in the same subnet, similar to EC2 instance-level security groups.
Option1: Configure security groups on Fargate task ENIs for task-level network access control
Option2: Use NACLs for task-level isolation
Option3: Fargate tasks share a single security group
Option4: Network isolation is not possible with Fargate

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides network traffic analysis to detect lateral movement and data exfiltration within their VPC. Which TWO services provide this? (Select TWO)
Explanation: GuardDuty analyzes VPC Flow Logs using ML to detect anomalous traffic patterns including lateral movement (internal port scanning, unusual internal traffic) and data exfiltration (large outbound transfers to unknown IPs). VPC Traffic Mirroring captures full packets for deep inspection by IDS/IPS appliances that can detect sophisticated attack patterns in packet content. Together they provide both behavioral (GuardDuty) and content-level (Traffic Mirroring) network threat detection.
Option1: GuardDuty for ML-based anomalous traffic pattern detection from VPC Flow Logs
Option2: VPC Traffic Mirroring with IDS appliances for deep packet inspection
Option3: Security groups for traffic analysis
Option4: CloudTrail for network traffic detection

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to implement a solution that provides secure DNS resolution for their VPC preventing DNS cache poisoning attacks. Which Route 53 feature provides this?
Explanation: Route 53 Resolver supports DNSSEC validation, which verifies the cryptographic signatures on DNS responses. When enabled, Resolver rejects DNS responses that fail signature validation, preventing cache poisoning and DNS spoofing attacks. DNSSEC validation is enabled on the Route 53 Resolver in the VPC settings. This provides authenticated DNS resolution for all VPC instances.
Option1: Enable DNSSEC validation on Route 53 Resolver for cryptographically authenticated DNS resolution
Option2: Use a custom DNS server without DNSSEC
Option3: Disable DNS resolution in the VPC
Option4: DNSSEC is not supported in AWS

QuestionText: A network engineer needs to design a solution that provides network connectivity for a large-scale data transfer between two AWS Regions. They need to transfer 100 TB with minimal cost. Which approach is MOST cost-effective?
Explanation: S3 Cross-Region Replication transfers data between Regions at standard cross-Region data transfer rates. For 100 TB, compressing data before transfer reduces volume and cost. S3 Transfer Acceleration can speed up the transfer but adds cost. For the most cost-effective approach, standard S3 replication with compressed data provides the best price-performance ratio for large cross-Region transfers.
Option1: Use S3 Cross-Region Replication with data compression for cost-effective large-scale cross-Region transfer
Option2: Use Direct Connect between Regions
Option3: Use Snowball for cross-Region transfer
Option4: Transfer data over the public internet
